mappings.rationaleAI: Behavioural ecology studies animal decision-making about feeding, mating, and territorial defence in ecological context, directly addressing the variety (A.1), classification (A.2), and components (A.3) of animal behaviour. Its integration of evolutionary biology and genetics with field observation bears on the evolution of behaviour and the influence of experience on behavioural patterns (D.2).

Covers the central and peripheral nervous systems, sensory and motor neurons, reflex arcs, neurotransmitters such as acetylcholine and dopamine, and the autonomic subdivision—directly addressing the nervous system's role in receiving information, processing it in brain and spinal cord, and initiating responses (C.3), reflex and reflexlike activities (B.2), and endocrine-nervous interaction (C.1).

Examines the endocrine system's chemical messengers including sex hormones (oestrogen, testosterone), stress hormones (adrenalin, cortisol), and feedback loops, directly relevant to hormonal influences on behaviour (C.2) and the interaction of endocrine and nervous systems (C.1).

Pheromones govern trail-laying, alarm responses, dominance hierarchies, sexual attraction, and caste determination in social insects—examples of fixed action patterns and instinctive behaviours (B.2). Their endocrine-like regulatory role connects to hormonal influences on behaviour (C.2), and their species-wide uniformity evidences genetic determination of behaviour (D.1).

Internal body clocks drive daily behavioural and physiological cycles across bacteria, plants, fungi, and animals, responding primarily to light-darkness cycles. Directly addresses photoperiodism (B.3), stereotyped plant responses to light (B.1), and rhythmic animal behavioural patterns as unlearned responses to environmental stimuli (B.2).

Bat and dolphin echolocation exemplifies specialized sensory-motor behaviour (A.1) involving fixed action patterns of sound emission and neural processing (B.2). Evolutionary arms races in which prey evolved frequency detection demonstrate genetic determination of behaviour (D.1) and co-evolutionary shaping of behavioural adaptations (D.2).

Brood parasitism by cuckoos and host behaviour modification by parasites illustrate fixed action patterns and instinctive responses (B.2) across diverse organisms (A.1). Co-evolutionary arms races between parasites and hosts demonstrate phyletic patterns in the evolution of behavioural adaptations (D.2).

Traces brain science from 19th-century speech localisation to modern neuroimaging, directly addressing how the nervous system processes information and initiates behavioural responses (C.3), including the biological mechanisms underlying thought and decision-making.
